concerns.text:
The proposed 'Cross-Modal Fusion' methodology (computing covariance between momentum spectra and polarization asymmetry from *independent* archival experiments) is physically invalid for deriving the D-coefficient. The D-coefficient ($ec{\sigma} \cdot (ec{p}_e 	imes ec{p}_
u)$) requires simultaneous measurement of electron momentum, neutrino momentum (recoil), and nuclear spin in a *single* decay event. Archival ENSDF data aggregates these into separate, independent measurements (spectra vs. asymmetry coefficients) with no event-level correlation. Computing covariance between these independent aggregates yields zero by definition (or noise), not a T-violation signal. No amount of permutation testing can recover a causal signal from uncorrelated independent variables. The research question cannot be answered by this method.

The plan assumes that 'Cross-Modal Fusion' of independent modalities can isolate T-odd terms. This violates the fundamental statistical requirement for covariance analysis: the variables must be measured on the same observational unit (event). Since the plan explicitly states data comes from 'independent modalities' (separate experiments/runs), the covariance matrix $\Sigma_{cross}$ will be a matrix of noise. The methodology cannot support the conclusion that 'archival data can reveal subtle violations' because the proposed statistical operation is mathematically incapable of detecting the target effect.
